Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. revision.text:
{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]]

Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]]

{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]]

In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. {{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]]

In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]]

{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Kyōgoku clan]] [[Category:Matsue-Matsudaira clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:Matsue Castle]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. {{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer revision.text:
{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[:en:Earthquake|Earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[:en:Earthquake|Earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. {{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[:en:Concrete|Concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[:en:Earthquake|Earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[:en:Horio Tadaharu|Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * revision.text:
{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[:en:Concrete|Concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[:en:Earthquake|Earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[:en:Horio Tadaharu|Horio Tadaharu]] and [[Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft revision.text:
{{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location=[[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The [[:en:Keep|Keep]] of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type=[[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder=[[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]] |materials= |height=30 [[metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
[[Kyōgoku clan]] (1633–1637)
[[Matsudaira clan]] (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]] located in [[Matsue, Shimane|Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by [[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the [[Matsue Domain]], during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Ownership was passed to the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] branch of the [[Kyōgoku clan|Kyōgoku]] in 1633 and then the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]], a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern [[:en:Concrete|Concrete]] reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived [[:en:Earthquake|Earthquake]]s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early [[Meiji period]], leaving only the [[:en:Keep|Keep]], an attached [[Turret (architecture)|turret]] and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of [[Lake Shinji]], is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the [[San'in region]]. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] region, [[:en:Horio Yoshiharu|Horio Yoshiharu]], and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of [[:en:Horio Tadaharu|Horio Tadaharu]] and [[:en:Kyōgoku Tadataka|Kyōgoku Tadataka]], [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from [[Matsumoto, Nagano|Matsumoto]] in [[Shinano Province]], and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category}} * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{draft Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] }} [[sn:Matsue Castle]] [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]]
