Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Category]] * [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location={{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep}} of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder={{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake|1=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location={{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}} of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder={{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan|1=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan|1=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain|1=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku|1=Kyōgoku clan}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete|1=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake|1=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji|1=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji|1=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder={{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan|1=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan|1=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain|1=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku|1=Kyōgoku clan}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete|1=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake|1=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji|1=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location={{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}} of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder={{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan|1=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan|1=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|WD=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain|1=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|WD=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku|1=Kyōgoku clan}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|WD=Q22657|lt=Concrete|1=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|WD=Q7944|lt=Earthquake|1=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|WD=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|WD=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|WD=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji|1=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|WD=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|WD=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|WD=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|WD=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Matsue Castle]] [[ar:قلعة ماتسويه]] [[ca:Castell de Matsue]] {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} [[cs:Macue (hrad)]] [[de:Burg Matsue]] [[eo:Macue (kastelo)]] [[es:Castillo Matsue]] [[eu:Matsue gaztelua]] [[fa:قلعه ماتسوئه]] [[fi:Matsuen linna]] [[fr:Château de Matsue]] [[he:טירת מצואה]] [[hu:Macuei várkastély]] [[hy:Մանցուե ամրոց]] [[id:Istana Matsue]] [[it:Castello di Matsue]] [[ja:松江城]] [[ko:마쓰에성]] [[ksh:Burch Matsue]] [[li:Kestiel van Matsue]] [[pam:Matsue (castillu)]] [[pl:Zamek Matsue]] [[pt:Castelo de Matsue]] [[ru:Замок Мацуэ]] [[sc:Casteddu de Matsue]] [[sv:Matsue slott]] [[th:ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ]] [[tl:Kastilyo ng Matsue]] [[tr:Matsue Kalesi]] [[uk:Замок Мацуе]] [[wuu:松江城]] [[zh:松江城]] [[zh-yue:松江城]] {{wikidata link|Q151776}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|qid=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. {{Short description|Castle in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox military installation |name=Matsue Castle |nativename-a=松江城 |partof= |location={{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|qid=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} |coordinates={{coord|35.474977|133.050556|format=dms|display=inline, title}} |map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan |map_size = |map_caption = |map_relief = 1 |image=File:080720 Matsue Castle Matsue Shimane pref Japan01s.jpg |image_size=300px |caption= The {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|qid=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}} of Matsue Castle in 2008 |type={{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} |code= |built=1607–{{start date and age|1611}} |builder={{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|qid=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}} |materials= |height=30 [[:en:metres|metres]] |used= |demolished= |condition= |ownership= |open_to_public= |controlledby=[[Horio clan]] (1611–1633)
{{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|qid=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku clan|1=Kyōgoku clan}} (1633–1637)
{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|qid=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan|1=Matsudaira clan}} (1637–1927)
City of Matsue (1927–present) |garrison= |current_commander= |past_commanders= |occupants= |battles= |events= }} {{nihongo|'''Matsue Castle'''|松江城|Matsue-jō}} is a {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|qid=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. Matsue Castle was constructed from 1607 to 1611 by {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|qid=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of the {{ill|Matsue Domain|en|Matsue Domain|qid=Q1017072|lt=Matsue Domain|1=Matsue Domain}}, during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Ownership was passed to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} branch of the {{ill|Kyōgoku clan|en|Kyōgoku clan|qid=Q2975299|lt=Kyōgoku|1=Kyōgoku clan}} in 1633 and then the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|qid=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}}, a junior branch of the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] clan, in 1637. The Matsudaira donated Matsue Castle to the city of Matsue in 1927. Matsue Castle is one of few remaining feudal Japanese castles that retains its main keep in its original wooden form and not a modern {{ill|Concrete|en|Concrete|qid=Q22657|lt=Concrete|1=Concrete}} reconstruction. Built after the last great war of [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the keep has survived {{ill|Earthquake|en|Earthquake|qid=Q7944|lt=Earthquake|1=Earthquake}}s, fires, wars and other causes that destroyed or damaged many Japanese castles. However, a number of its castle buildings were demolished during the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, leaving only the {{ill|Keep|en|Keep|qid=Q91165|lt=Keep|1=Keep}}, an attached {{ill|Turret (architecture)|en|Turret (architecture)|qid=Q6502179|lt=turret|1=Turret (architecture)}} and stone walls existing as original structures today, though some of the other castle buildings have been reconstructed in modern times. Matsue Castle, standing on the shores of {{ill|Lake Shinji|en|Lake Shinji|qid=Q1151248|lt=Lake Shinji|1=Lake Shinji}}, is one of Japan's Three Great Lake Castles and the heart of Matsue's central riverside district.{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/shimane/matsue-castle/information|title=About Matsue Castle - Shimane Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2018-02-13}} [[File:Matsue castle outside and inside - 2019 1 4.webm|thumb|thumbtime=1|Matsue Castle outside and inside, 2019]] ==History== Of the 100+ castles remaining in Japan, Matsue Castle is the only one with a surviving main keep in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|qid=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}. This keep is the second largest, the third tallest (30m) and the sixth oldest amongst Japanese castles. It was built over a period of 5 years by the daimyō of the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} region, {{ill|Horio Yoshiharu|en|Horio Yoshiharu|qid=Q8190645|lt=Horio Yoshiharu|1=Horio Yoshiharu}}, and was completed in 1611. After the reigns of {{ill|Horio Tadaharu|en|Horio Tadaharu|qid=Q5903277|lt=Horio Tadaharu|1=Horio Tadaharu}} and {{ill|Kyōgoku Tadataka|en|Kyōgoku Tadataka|qid=Q11374423|lt=Kyōgoku Tadataka|1=Kyōgoku Tadataka}}, [[Matsudaira Naomasa]], a grandson of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, became Lord of the castle, after being transferred from {{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|en|Matsumoto, Nagano|qid=Q213324|lt=Matsumoto|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and thus began a reign that lasted 10 generations of the Matsudaira clan over a period of 234 years. In 1875, all of the buildings within the castle were dismantled, with the exception of the castle tower itself and attached turret, which were allowed to remain due to pressure from interest groups. These buildings underwent a complete reconstruction between 1950 and 1955. In 2001, several of the castle's former turrets were reconstructed.https://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Matsue {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}} The keep is a complex structure, built in a watchtower-style, that appears to be five stories from the outside, but has, in fact, six levels inside. Most of the walls of the keep are painted black. It is a strong structure, built to withstand warfare, yet at the same time, it is majestic and solemn, reminiscent of the Momoyama style. The tenshu and attached turret of Matsue Castle have been registered as a [[:en:national treasure of Japan|national treasure of Japan]] since July 9, 2015.http://tabijikan.com/article/17201/ | Matsue Castle - finally designed as a Japan's national treasure in 2015 | Retrieved 17 Jan 2017. ==Gallery== {{Gallery | title = | align = | footer = | style = | state = | height = | width = | captionstyle = | File:Matsue Castle Ninomaru.jpg | alt1= | ''Ninomaru'' | File:Matsue Castle 03.JPG | alt2= | Ruins of the Ote gate | File:Matsue castle interior.jpg | alt3= | Castle interior }} ==References== {{Reflist}} == Literature == *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=374 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book| title=Castles in Japan| last=Schmorleitz| first=Morton S.| year=1974| publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co.| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-8048-1102-4| url-access=registration| url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm}} *{{cite book | title=Japanese Castles| last=Motoo| first=Hinago| year=1986| publisher=Kodansha| location=Tokyo| isbn=0-87011-766-1| page= 200 pages}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| ISBN=978-1568365121}} == External links == {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5801.html Japan Guide] * [http://www.visit-matsue.com/discover/north_side.html#matsue_castle Visit Matsue] * [http://www.matsue-tourism.or.jp/m_castle/ Matsue Tourism] * [https://www.tripadvisor.com.sg/Attraction_Review-g298136-d324845-Reviews-Matsue_Castle-Matsue_Shimane_Prefecture_Chugoku.html Tripadvisor] {{100 Fine Castles of Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{Commons category|Matsue Castle}} {{wikidata link|Q151776|ar|قلعة ماتسويه|ca|Castell de Matsue|co|commons:Category:Matsue Castle|cs|Macue (hrad)|de|Burg Matsue|en|Matsue Castle|eo|Kastelo de Macuo|es|Castillo Matsue|eu|Matsue gaztelua|fa|قلعه ماتسوئه|fi|Matsuen linna|fr|Château de Matsue|he|טירת מצואה|hu|Macuei várkastély|hy|Մանցուե ամրոց|id|Kastel Matsue|it|Castello di Matsue|ja|松江城|ko|마쓰에성|ksh|Burch Matsue|li|Kestiel van Matsue|pam|Matsue (castillu)|pl|Zamek Matsue|pt|Castelo de Matsue|ru|Замок Мацуэ|sc|Casteddu de Matsue|sv|Matsue slott|th|ปราสาทมัตสึเอะ|tl|Kastilyo ng Matsue|tr|Matsue Kalesi|uk|Замок Мацуе|wuu|松江城|zh-yue|松江城|zh|松江城|eo|Macue (kastelo)|id|Istana Matsue}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsue]] [[Category:Castles in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History museums in Japan]] [[Category:Museums in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with interwikis with duplicate languages]]
